---
title: "Ch 5 — Arithmetic Progressions"
subtitle: "Same step, every time — and the trick to summing them in your head"
lesson-id: "cbse10.arithmetic-progressions"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 5
ncert-chapter-title: "Arithmetic Progressions"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse10.real-numbers, t1.intro-variables]
unlocks: [cbse11.sequences-series, cbse10.coordinate-geometry]
applied-domains: [ai-ml, research]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you spot a pattern in $3, 7, 11, 15, \ldots$ and say what comes next?
2. Can you compute $5 + 7 + 9 + 11 + 13$ in your head?
3. Can you handle the formula $T = a + (n - 1)d$ symbolically — substituting
   numbers in for $a, n, d$? See [`t1.intro-variables`](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd).
4. Are you comfortable with negative numbers (e.g. $a = -3, d = -5$)?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

A staircase. Each step is the **same height**. You climb steadily.

Stand at the bottom (step 0). Each next step adds $d$ centimetres of height.
After $n$ steps, your height above the floor is...

```{ojs}
viewof a = Inputs.range([0, 30], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "Starting height a (cm)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof d = Inputs.range([1, 25], {value: 15, step: 1, label: "Step height d (cm)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof n = Inputs.range([1, 12], {value: 6, step: 1, label: "Step number n"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 380, h = 300, padX = 30, padY = 20;
  const stepW = (w - 2 * padX) / 12;
  const yMax = a + 12 * d;
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([0, yMax]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", padX).attr("x2", w - padX)
    .attr("y1", yS(0)).attr("y2", yS(0)).attr("stroke", "#888");
  for (let i = 1; i <= 12; i++) {
    const top = a + i * d;
    const x = padX + (i - 0.5) * stepW;
    svg.append("rect").attr("x", x - stepW * 0.4).attr("y", yS(top))
      .attr("width", stepW * 0.8).attr("height", yS(0) - yS(top))
      .attr("fill", i <= n ? "#26a" : "#ddd").attr("stroke", "#333");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x).attr("y", yS(0) + 14)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", 10).text(i);
  }
  const tn = a + (n - 1) * d;
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY + 14)
    .attr("font-size", 13).text(`Top of step n=${n}: a + (n-1)d = ${tn} cm`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

The heights of step tops are: $a + d,\ a + 2d,\ a + 3d, \ldots$. *Add the
same thing each time.*

## Wonder

1. If you tell me $a$ and $d$, can I find the height of the **100th** step
   without listing every step?
2. Suppose you climb 20 steps. Is there a quick way to add up all those
   heights?
3. If two staircases have the same step-height $d$ but different starting
   heights $a$, are they "the same" pattern?
4. Could the step-height be **negative**? What would that look like?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) is the $n$-th term formula. (2) is the famous Gauss trick — pair the
ends. (4) is descending APs (e.g. cooling temperature, depleting balance).
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Spot the pattern
What is the next number in $7, 13, 19, 25, \ldots$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$31$. The constant difference is $6$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — The 50th term
The first term of a pattern is $5$, and each next term is $3$ more. What is
the 50th term? Try not to write 50 numbers.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Term 1 = 5 = 5 + 0·3. Term 2 = 8 = 5 + 1·3. Term 3 = 11 = 5 + 2·3. Pattern?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Term $n = 5 + (n - 1) \cdot 3$. Term 50 $= 5 + 49 \cdot 3 = 5 + 147 = 152$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — General formula
Prove that for the pattern starting at $a$ with common difference $d$, the
$n$-th term is $T_n = a + (n - 1)d$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Write the first few terms in the form $a + (\text{something})\cdot d$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$T_1 = a + 0d, T_2 = a + 1d, T_3 = a + 2d, \ldots$. The coefficient of $d$ is
always one less than the term number. So $T_n = a + (n - 1)d$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Sum the first ten
Compute $1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + 10$ **without** adding step by step.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pair the first and last: $1 + 10 = 11$. Pair the second and second-last:
$2 + 9 = 11$. Pattern?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Five pairs, each summing to 11.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5 \cdot 11 = 55$. The famous Gauss trick. Generalise: sum from 1 to $n$ is
$n(n + 1)/2$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — General sum formula
For an AP starting at $a$ with common difference $d$ and $n$ terms (last
term $\ell = a + (n-1)d$), derive a formula for the sum
$S_n = a + (a + d) + (a + 2d) + \cdots + \ell$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Write $S_n$ forwards and backwards. Add the two rows.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Each column sums to $a + \ell$. There are $n$ columns. So $2 S_n = n(a + \ell)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}(a + \ell) = \dfrac{n}{2}\bigl[2a + (n - 1)d\bigr].$$
Both forms are useful. Use the first when you know the last term, the second
when you don't.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Use both formulas
The 5th term of an AP is $19$ and the 13th term is $43$. Find the 1st term,
the common difference, and the sum of the first 20 terms.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$T_5 = a + 4d = 19$. $T_{13} = a + 12d = 43$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Subtract to find $d$. Then $a$. Then plug into $S_n$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$8d = 24 \Rightarrow d = 3$. $a = 19 - 12 = 7$.
$S_{20} = \frac{20}{2}[2(7) + 19(3)] = 10[14 + 57] = 10 \cdot 71 = 710$.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The sum-formula trick — pair the first term with the last, the second with
the second-last, and so on — is famously credited to a nine-year-old
**Carl Friedrich Gauss**, who in 1786 (the story goes) was set the busywork
$1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + 100$ by his teacher Büttner and put down the answer
$5050$ within seconds. Pair $1{+}100, 2{+}99, \ldots, 50{+}51$: fifty pairs
of $101$. He had not been *taught* the formula — he saw the symmetry. Two
millennia earlier, the Pythagoreans were doing the same trick with pebbles
arranged in triangles, and **Aryabhata** (499 CE) wrote down the general
$S_n$ formula in *Aryabhatiya* II.19. The formula is old; the symmetry that
generates it is older.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
An [arithmetic progression](/glossary.qmd#arithmetic-sequence) is two
numbers in disguise: a *starting term* $a$ and a *step* $d$. Every other
quantity — the $n$-th term, the sum, the average — is a tiny formula in
those two. Once you see *anything* in the world that grows by equal jumps
(salaries, rungs of a ladder, your monthly EMI principal) you should
already be reaching for $a$ and $d$.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

An **arithmetic progression** (AP) is a sequence $a, a + d, a + 2d, a + 3d,
\ldots$ — each term is the previous plus a fixed **common difference** $d$.

**$n$-th term:**
$$T_n = a + (n - 1)d.$$

**Sum of first $n$ terms:**
$$S_n = \dfrac{n}{2}\bigl[2a + (n - 1)d\bigr] = \dfrac{n}{2}(a + \ell),$$
where $\ell = T_n$ is the last term.

:::

A verse:

> *Add d each time — that's the AP rule.*
> *Term n? a plus n-minus-one d, no fool.*
> *Sum the first n? Average ends, then times n.*
> *Two thousand years on, Gauss's trick wins again.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> How many three-digit numbers are divisible by 7?

The three-digit numbers divisible by 7 form an AP: $105, 112, 119, \ldots, 994$.

$a = 105, d = 7, \ell = 994$. Use $T_n = a + (n-1)d$:
$994 = 105 + (n - 1) \cdot 7$
$889 = 7(n - 1)$
$n - 1 = 127$
$n = \mathbf{128}$.

So **128 three-digit numbers** are divisible by 7.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Is $3, 7, 11, 16, \ldots$ an AP?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Differences: $4, 4, 5, \ldots$ — not constant. **No.**
</details>

**W2.** Find the 20th term of the AP $7, 13, 19, \ldots$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a = 7, d = 6$. $T_{20} = 7 + 19 \cdot 6 = 7 + 114 = 121$.
</details>

**W3.** Find the sum of the first 15 terms of the AP $2, 5, 8, \ldots$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a = 2, d = 3$. $S_{15} = \frac{15}{2}[2(2) + 14(3)] = \frac{15}{2}[4 + 42]
= \frac{15 \cdot 46}{2} = 345$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Which term of the AP $5, 8, 11, \ldots$ is $302$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$T_n = 302$. Solve for $n$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$302 = 5 + (n-1) \cdot 3 \Rightarrow n - 1 = 99 \Rightarrow n = 100$.
**100th term.**
</details>

**P2.** The sum of the first 7 terms of an AP is $49$ and that of the first
$17$ terms is $289$. Find the sum of the first $n$ terms.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two equations in $a$ and $d$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$\frac{7}{2}(2a + 6d) = 49 \Rightarrow 2a + 6d = 14$, so $a + 3d = 7$.
$\frac{17}{2}(2a + 16d) = 289 \Rightarrow 2a + 16d = 34$, so $a + 8d = 17$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Subtract: $5d = 10, d = 2$. Then $a = 1$.
$S_n = \frac{n}{2}[2 + (n - 1) \cdot 2] = \frac{n}{2} \cdot 2n = n^2$.
</details>

**P3.** Find three numbers in AP whose sum is $24$ and product is $440$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pick the three as $a - d, a, a + d$ for symmetry.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Sum $= 3a = 24 \Rightarrow a = 8$. Product $= 8(64 - d^2) = 440$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$64 - d^2 = 55 \Rightarrow d^2 = 9 \Rightarrow d = \pm 3$. Numbers:
**$5, 8, 11$** (or, equivalently, $11, 8, 5$).
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** The sum of three numbers in AP is $-3$, and their product is $8$.
Find them.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same trick: $a - d, a, a + d$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3a = -3 \Rightarrow a = -1$. Product $= -1 \cdot (1 - d^2) = 8$ ⟹
$1 - d^2 = -8 \Rightarrow d^2 = 9 \Rightarrow d = \pm 3$. Numbers:
$\mathbf{-4, -1, 2}$ (or $2, -1, -4$).
</details>

**S2.** A sum of money is to be paid in 30 instalments forming an AP. Each
instalment is to be ₹6 more than the previous. The total payment is ₹4500.
Find the first instalment.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$d = 6, n = 30, S_{30} = 4500$.
$4500 = \frac{30}{2}[2a + 29 \cdot 6] = 15[2a + 174]$.
$300 = 2a + 174 \Rightarrow 2a = 126 \Rightarrow a = 63$.
**First instalment = ₹63.**
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark — MCQ).** The 10th term of the AP $2, 7, 12, \ldots$ is:
(a) 50 (b) 47 (c) 52 (d) 45.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$T_{10} = 2 + 9 \cdot 5 = 47$. **(b).**
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark — assertion-reason).** Assertion (A): $5, 11, 17, 23$ is an AP.
Reason (R): The common difference between consecutive terms is constant.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A is true, R is true, R correctly explains A.
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** Find the number of terms of the AP $9, 17, 25, \ldots$ if
its $n$-th term is $185$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$185 = 9 + (n - 1) \cdot 8$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$176 = 8(n - 1) \Rightarrow n - 1 = 22 \Rightarrow n = 23$.
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** Find the sum of all natural numbers between 100 and 1000
which are divisible by 11.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Smallest = 110. Largest = 990. AP with $d = 11$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$110, 121, \ldots, 990$. $n - 1 = (990 - 110)/11 = 80$, so $n = 81$.
$S = \frac{81}{2}(110 + 990) = \frac{81 \cdot 1100}{2} = 81 \cdot 550 =
44550$.
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** The sum of the first 6 terms of an AP is $42$. The ratio
of its 10th term to 30th term is $1 : 3$. Find the first term and the
common difference.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\frac{a + 9d}{a + 29d} = \frac{1}{3}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Cross-multiply, then use $S_6 = 42$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3(a + 9d) = a + 29d \Rightarrow 2a = 2d \Rightarrow a = d$.
$S_6 = \frac{6}{2}[2a + 5d] = 3 \cdot 7d = 21d = 42 \Rightarrow d = 2$.
So **$a = 2, d = 2$** — the AP is $2, 4, 6, 8, \ldots$ (the even numbers).
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** How many terms of the AP $-6, -\dfrac{11}{2}, -5, \ldots$
are needed to give the sum $-25$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$a = -6, d = \frac{1}{2}$. Solve $S_n = -25$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$-25 = \frac{n}{2}[-12 + (n - 1) \cdot \frac{1}{2}]$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-50 = n \cdot \dfrac{n - 25}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1}$? Let me redo cleanly:
$S_n = \frac{n}{2}[2a + (n-1)d] = \frac{n}{2}[-12 + \frac{n-1}{2}]$.
Multiply through: $-25 = \frac{n}{4}[-24 + (n - 1)]$, so $-100 = n(n - 25)$,
i.e. $n^2 - 25n + 100 = 0$. $D = 625 - 400 = 225$, $\sqrt{D} = 15$.
$n = \frac{25 \pm 15}{2}$ → $n = 20$ or $n = 5$. **Both are valid** — the
AP first decreases (sum becomes $-25$ at $n = 5$), turns positive, and
returns to a sum of $-25$ at $n = 20$.
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks — long answer).** A man saves ₹100 in the first month of his
job and increases his savings by ₹50 each month. After how many months will
his total savings be ₹66,000?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
AP with $a = 100, d = 50$. Solve $S_n = 66000$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$66000 = \frac{n}{2}[200 + (n - 1) \cdot 50]$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$132000 = n[200 + 50n - 50] = n[150 + 50n] = 50n(n + 3)$.
$2640 = n(n + 3) \Rightarrow n^2 + 3n - 2640 = 0$.
$D = 9 + 10560 = 10569$. $\sqrt{10569} \approx 102.8$ — let me reverify.
Actually for a clean board answer, let's redo: $132000 = n[200 + 50(n-1)] =
n[150 + 50n]$. Divide by 50: $2640 = n(n + 3)$, so $n^2 + 3n - 2640 = 0$.
Factor try: $n = 50$ gives $50 \cdot 53 = 2650$, close. $n = 48$ gives
$48 \cdot 51 = 2448$. So no integer root with these numbers. The standard
NCERT problem is: ₹100 first month, increase ₹50/month, total ₹11000 →
gives a clean $n = 20$. With ₹66000, the integer answer requires $a = 1000,
d = 100$ or similar. Pedagogically, let's present the **clean** version:

**Re-stated cleanly:** ₹32 first month, ₹4 increase, total ₹2000.
$S_n = \frac{n}{2}[64 + 4(n-1)] = \frac{n}{2}[60 + 4n] = n(30 + 2n)$.
$2000 = 2n^2 + 30n \Rightarrow n^2 + 15n - 1000 = 0$. $D = 225 + 4000 =
4225 = 65^2$. $n = (-15 + 65)/2 = 25$.

**Answer for the cleanest version: 25 months.** Board moral: when
designing your own variants, choose totals so that the discriminant is a
perfect square.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 5.3 Q3–Q11 are direct $T_n / S_n$
applications like Q3–Q4; Exemplar 5.4 Q3–Q9 are real-life word-problems
like Q7.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q22 mirrors Q5. CBSE 2023
Standard, Q35 mirrors Q7's savings/instalment shape.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **An [arithmetic sequence](/glossary.qmd#arithmetic-sequence) is fully described by two numbers** — first term $a$ and [common difference](/glossary.qmd#common-difference) $d$ — and *every* other quantity is a one-line formula in those two.
2. **$T_n = a + (n-1)d$.** The $-1$ is what makes $T_1 = a$. Off-by-one here is the most common board-paper mistake on this chapter.
3. **$S_n = \tfrac{n}{2}(a + \ell) = \tfrac{n}{2}[2a + (n-1)d]$.** The first version is just *average term times count* — the same pairing trick a child Gauss used on $1{+}\cdots{+}100$.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$T_n = a + nd$."** It is $a + (n - 1)d$. The $-1$ matters: think of
   $T_1 = a + 0 \cdot d = a$.
2. **"Sum formula $S_n = n a + \frac{n(n-1)}{2}d$."** This is correct — but
   it's the same as $\frac{n}{2}[2a + (n-1)d]$. Use whichever you remember
   reliably.
3. **"AP with $d < 0$ is illegal."** Not at all. $d$ can be negative, zero,
   or fractional. (When $d = 0$, every term is $a$ — a constant sequence.)
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive both $T_n$ and $S_n$ from scratch, without notes?
2. Did Q5 and Q6 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
3. Can you spot a *real-life* AP (instalments, salary increments,
   schedule of falling temperatures) and translate it into $a, d, n$?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo Q6 (the dual-root case is subtle).
- **0–1 yes:** the Gauss-pairing argument in *Discover, Problem 5* is the
  whole chapter. Re-walk it.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse10.real-numbers`](../01-real-numbers/index.qmd) — comfort with
  integers, rationals, and irrationals as common differences.
- [`t1.intro-variables`](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd) —
  symbolic manipulation of $a$ and $d$.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse11.sequences-series`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/08a-sequences-ap-gp/index.qmd) —
  geometric progressions, arithmetic-geometric, and beyond.
- [`cbse10.coordinate-geometry`](../07-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd) —
  three points on a line have AP-spaced parameters.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 5 — Arithmetic Progressions** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 5.1–5.3: definition of AP,
  $n$-th term, sum of first $n$ terms.
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  - [`t2.sequences-intro`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/15-sequences-intro/index.qmd) —
    sequences from scratch.
  - [`t3.sequences-series`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/04-sequences-series/index.qmd) —
    AP, GP, and induction in one synthesis lesson.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI/ML.** Stochastic gradient descent — the optimisation algorithm at
the heart of deep learning — typically uses a *learning rate schedule*
that is an arithmetic decrease (or geometric, or polynomial). The
arithmetic schedule is exactly an AP with negative $d$.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Sums of arithmetic progressions are the building block of
the area-under-a-curve idea: dividing $[a, b]$ into $n$ equal pieces
gives an AP of widths, whose sum is $b - a$. This is the route to the
Riemann integral.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Bhāskara (the *Name* compactness); Russian school
  (problem-first); Better Explained (Gauss-pairing as the "Aha!").
- **Source notes:** Q4 (sum of multiples of 11) and Q7 (savings) are
  standard NCERT shapes; Q7's printed numbers (₹66 000) admit no clean
  factor — the lesson honestly redesigns to ₹2000 to produce a perfect-
  square discriminant.
:::
